module.title: ENUMERABLE
module.subtitle: reduce · group_by · sort_by · min_by/max_by
module.theory:
## The Enumerable toolkit

Every array (and hash) in Ruby mixes in **Enumerable** — a giant box of looping tools. You almost never write a manual `for` loop. Instead you pick the tool that already does what you want and hand it a **block**: `{ |x| ... }` for one line, or `do |x| ... end` for many.

The golden rule of blocks: **the last line of the block is its value**. No `return` needed — and `return` inside a block is actually a mistake that jumps out of the whole method.

## reduce / inject — fold a list into one value

`reduce` walks the list carrying an **accumulator**. The block's result becomes the next accumulator.
~~~ruby
[1, 2, 3, 4].reduce(0) { |sum, n| sum + n }   # 10
[1, 2, 3, 4].reduce(1) { |prod, n| prod * n } # 24
~~~
The number in `reduce(0)` is the **starting** accumulator. For a product, start at **1** (starting at 0 would zero everything out — a classic beginner trap). There's even a shortcut for simple operators: `[1,2,3].reduce(:+)  # 6`.

> INTEL — `inject` is the exact same method under a different name. `reduce` and `inject` are interchangeable.

## each_with_object — build a collection as you go

When you want to fill up a hash or array, `each_with_object` is cleaner than `reduce`. You pass the object **once**, and it is handed back to you every loop:
~~~ruby
["a", "b", "a"].each_with_object(Hash.new(0)) do |word, counts|
  counts[word] += 1
end
# { "a" => 2, "b" => 1 }
~~~

> WARNING — `each_with_object` **returns the object you passed in**, not the block's last line. So you mutate `counts` (e.g. `counts[word] += 1`) and let Ruby hand the finished object back. Trying to `return counts` from inside, or writing the count as the last line, are the two most common slip-ups.

## group_by — bucket items by a key

Gives you a **hash** mapping each block result to the array of items that produced it.
~~~ruby
[1, 2, 3, 4, 5].group_by { |n| n.even? }
# { false => [1, 3, 5], true => [2, 4] }
~~~

## sort_by — order by a computed value

Sorts ascending by whatever the block returns. To reverse, negate a number or call `.reverse` after.
~~~ruby
["ruby", "go", "perl"].sort_by { |w| w.length }   # ["go", "ruby", "perl"]
~~~

## min_by / max_by — the single best item

Returns the actual **element** whose block value is smallest / largest — not the number itself.
~~~ruby
["ruby", "go", "perl"].max_by { |w| w.length }   # "perl"
~~~

## count, find / detect

`count { |x| ... }` counts how many items match. `find` (alias `detect`) returns the **first** matching element, or `nil` if none.
~~~ruby
[1, 2, 3, 4].count { |n| n.even? }     # 2
[1, 2, 3, 4].find { |n| n > 2 }        # 3
~~~

> INTEL — Chaining is the whole game: `select` then `map` is the most common combo — filter first, then transform. `data.select { |x| x.live? }.map { |x| x.name }` reads top-to-bottom like a pipeline.

> WARNING — Symbol keys vs string keys are different worlds. A hash written `{ bpm: 128 }` is read with `h[:bpm]` (symbol), while `{ "bpm" => 128 }` needs `h["bpm"]` (string). Mixing them up returns `nil`.

exercises.title: SUM CORE
exercises.prompt:
Define **fold_sum(nums)** that uses **reduce** to add up every number in the array and return the total. Start the accumulator at **0**.
~~~ruby
fold_sum([1, 2, 3, 4])  # 10
~~~
exercises.starter:
def fold_sum(nums)
  # TODO: use reduce starting at 0
  0
end
exercises.lore: One value to bind them all.

exercises.title: REACTOR PRODUCT
exercises.prompt:
Define **chain_product(nums)** that uses **reduce** to multiply every number together. Start the accumulator at **1** so an empty array returns 1.
~~~ruby
chain_product([2, 3, 4])  # 24
~~~
exercises.starter:
def chain_product(nums)
  # TODO: reduce with a starting value
  0
end
exercises.hint: Start at 1, not 0: nums.reduce(1) { |prod, n| prod * n }
exercises.lore: Multiply the load until the core hums.

exercises.title: PACKET TALLY
exercises.brief: Count how often each tag appears.
exercises.prompt:
Define **tally(words)** that returns a **hash** mapping each string to how many times it appears. Use **each_with_object** with a `Hash.new(0)` so missing keys default to 0.
~~~ruby
tally(["a", "b", "a"])  # { "a" => 2, "b" => 1 }
~~~
exercises.solution:
def tally(words)
  words.each_with_object(Hash.new(0)) do |w, counts|
    counts[w] += 1
  end
end
exercises.lore: Every packet logged, none lost in the noise.

exercises.title: PARITY BUCKETS
exercises.brief: Split numbers into even and odd.
exercises.prompt:
Define **by_parity(nums)** that uses **group_by** to bucket numbers by whether they are even. Return the hash keyed by **true** (even) and **false** (odd).
~~~ruby
by_parity([1, 2, 3, 4])  # { false => [1, 3], true => [2, 4] }
~~~
exercises.lore: Sort the signal from the static.

exercises.title: MAX SIGNAL
exercises.prompt:
Define **longest(words)** that uses **max_by** to return the **string** with the most characters. If two tie, returning the first one is fine. Assume the array is never empty.
~~~ruby
longest(["go", "ruby", "kernel"])  # "kernel"
~~~
exercises.starter:
def longest(words)
  # TODO: max_by length
  words.first
end

tests.name: returns the element not its length

exercises.lore: The loudest broadcast wins the channel.

exercises.title: RANK BY BPM
exercises.prompt:
**tracks** is an array of hashes like **{ name: "Spice", bpm: 128 }**. Define **ranked_names(tracks)** returning an array of the **names** sorted by **bpm** ascending. Use **sort_by** then **map**.
~~~ruby
ranked_names([{ name: "Fast", bpm: 140 }, { name: "Slow", bpm: 90 }])  # ["Slow", "Fast"]
~~~

tests.name: sorts by bpm
tests.code:
data = [{ name: "Fast", bpm: 140 }, { name: "Slow", bpm: 90 }, { name: "Mid", bpm: 120 }]
assert_equal(ranked_names(data), ["Slow", "Mid", "Fast"])

exercises.lore: Line them up by tempo before the set.

exercises.title: FIRST HIT
exercises.prompt:
Define **first_over(nums, limit)** that uses **find** (a.k.a. detect) to return the **first** number strictly greater than **limit**. Return **nil** if none qualify.
~~~ruby
first_over([1, 5, 8, 2], 4)  # 5
first_over([1, 2], 9)        # nil
~~~
exercises.starter:
def first_over(nums, limit)
  # TODO: find the first number over the limit
  nums.first
end
exercises.lore: Lock on the first heat signature.

exercises.title: SQUAD REPORT
exercises.prompt:
**units** is an array of hashes like **{ name: "Trin", hp: 80, online: true }**. Define **report(units)** returning a **hash** with three keys:
- **:online** — how many units have `online: true` (use **count**)
- **:total_hp** — the sum of `hp` across the online units only (use **select** then **reduce** or **sum**)
- **:strongest** — the **name** of the online unit with the highest hp (use **max_by**)

Assume at least one unit is online.
~~~ruby
report([{ name: "Trin", hp: 80, online: true }, { name: "Dozer", hp: 50, online: false }])
# { online: 1, total_hp: 80, strongest: "Trin" }
~~~

tests.name: full report
tests.code:
data = [{ name: "Trin", hp: 80, online: true }, { name: "Dozer", hp: 50, online: false }, { name: "Neo", hp: 95, online: true }]
assert_equal(report(data), { online: 2, total_hp: 175, strongest: "Neo" })

tests.name: ignores offline in hp + strongest
tests.code:
data = [{ name: "A", hp: 10, online: true }, { name: "B", hp: 999, online: false }]
assert_equal(report(data), { online: 1, total_hp: 10, strongest: "A" })

tests.name: strongest is the online max
tests.code:
data = [{ name: "Lo", hp: 30, online: true }, { name: "Hi", hp: 70, online: true }]
r = report(data)
assert_equal(r[:strongest], "Hi")
assert_equal(r[:online], 2)

exercises.hint: live = units.select { |u| u[:online] }; then count, reduce hp, and max_by hp on live.
exercises.lore: Only the jacked-in count toward the run.
